coredata.dc:title: Bright-green-emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots as a nanoprobe for bifunctional sensing, its logic gate operation and cellular imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Du, Fangfang
Gong, Xiaojuan
Lu, Wenjing
Liu, Yang
Gao, Yifang
Shuang, Shaomin
Xian, Ming
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: A fluorescent nanoprobe based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with green fluorescent emission have been fabricated through a facile one-step hydrothermal treatment using catechol and triethylene tetramine as precursors. The obtained N-CDs with excellent luminescent properties and superior biocompatibility have been applied for the development of a bifunctional sensor for the detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid (AA). Fe3+ that are tightly chelating the surface of N-CDs can induce fluorescence (FL) quenching of N-CDs through photo-induced electron transfer (PET). Meanwhile, the addition of AA serves to shelter the CDs effectively from being quenched by Fe3+ due to AA, as an antioxidant, enable easy-conversion of Fe3+ to reduced states (i.e. Fe3+ and Fe2+). The N-CDs are used as a facile and label-free “on-off-on” fluorescent nanoprobe for the determination of Fe3+ and AA with detection limits of 58.82nM and 0.236µM with the corresponding linear ranges of 25–200µM and 25–300µM, respectively. According to this phenomenon, an “AND” logic gate based on the novel N-CDs has been constructed. As-prepared N-CDs with negligible toxicity and perfect biocompatibility were expanded for cellular imaging and sensing Fe3+ and AA in living cell, which enlarges the application range of the N-CDs. Most importantly, the as-constructed fluorescent sensing system was successfully applied to detection of Fe3+ in tap water and the analyses of AA in fresh fruits with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Bifunctional sensing
Logic gate
Cellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorimetry for the determination of Fe3+and AA
Detection of AA in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21705101 , 21475080 and 21575084 ) and Shanxi Province Hundred Talents Project . Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and HRTEM images, (b) size distribution of N-doped CDs.
(a) XPS survey scan of N-CDs, (b) C1s XPS, (c) N1s XPS and (d) FTIR spectrum of N-CDs.

(a) The optical properties of N-CDs aqueous solutions. UV–Vis absorption spectra (inset: photographs taken under visible light). Optimal excitation and emission PL spectra (inset: photographs taken under 365nm UV ligtht). (b) FL spectra of N-CDs (2mgmL−1).with different λex 360–460nm.
(a) FL spectra of N-CDs (black line) and with 500µM Fe3+ (red line). (b) Absorbance of N-CDs (black line) and with 100µM Fe3+ (red line).
Fluorescence titration spectra of CDs upon the addition of Fe3+ (0–500µM). Inset: The fluorescence intensity of CDs as a function of Fe3+ concentration.

(a) Photographs of N-CDs in the absence of Fe3+ and AA (first), in the presence of Fe3+ (second) and in the presence of Fe3+ and AA (third) under visible light and UV light. (b) Fluorescence response of the N-CDs/Fe3+ mixture to AA at various concentrations (0–700µM). Inset: Dependence of F0-F on the various concentrations of AA (25–200µM). (c) Fluorescence intensity response of N-CDs/Fe3+ mixture to 0.7mM AA and other analytes (2-fold concentration of AA). (d) Fluorescence intensity response of N-CDs/Fe3+ mixture to AA and other analytes (0.7mM) in the presence of AA (0.7mM). F0 is fluorescence intensity of N-CDs/Fe3+ mixture, and F is fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs/Fe3+ mixture in the presence of AA or interferants.
Truth table (top) and the combinatorial logic scheme (bottom) of “AND” logic operations.
Effect of on MCF-7 cells viability. (mean% ± SD, n = 6).

LSCM images of MCF-7 cells cultured in 0.60mgmL−1 N-CDs/0.5mMMn+ (Mn+ including (1) blank, (2) K+, (3) Na+, (4) Zn2+, (5) Mg2+, (6) Cu2+, (7) Ca2+, (8) Mn2+ and (9) Fe2+) at 37℃ for 2h (1A-9A), then subjected to 0.50mM Fe3+ (1B-9B) and finally cultured in 0.7mM AA (1C-9C). (D) Intracellular fluorescence responses of N-CDs+Mn+, N-CDs+Mn++Fe3+ and N-CDs+Mn++Fe3++AA. Data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation. The intensities of 25 cells were measured. λex/λem are 488/500 ± 25nm. Scale bar, 20µm.
Illustration of the synthetic procedure and cellular imaging for N-CDs and the N-CDs-based bifunctional fluorescence nanoprobe for of Fe3+ and AA.
Schematic illustration of detection strategy of AA based on N–CDs.
Analytical results for the detection of Fe3+ in tap water samples.
Analytical results for the detection of AA in fresh fruits.

head.ce:title: Bright-green-emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots as a nanoprobe for bifunctional sensing, its logic gate operation and cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Washington State University, Pullman, WA, 99164, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Washington State University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescent nanoprobe based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with green fluorescent emission have been fabricated through a facile one-step hydrothermal treatment using catechol and triethylene tetramine as precursors. The obtained N-CDs with excellent luminescent properties and superior biocompatibility have been applied for the development of a bifunctional sensor for the detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid (AA). Fe3+ that are tightly chelating the surface of N-CDs can induce fluorescence (FL) quenching of N-CDs through photo-induced electron transfer (PET). Meanwhile, the addition of AA serves to shelter the CDs effectively from being quenched by Fe3+ due to AA, as an antioxidant, enable easy-conversion of Fe3+ to reduced states (i.e. Fe3+ and Fe2+). The N-CDs are used as a facile and label-free “on-off-on” fluorescent nanoprobe for the determination of Fe3+ and AA with detection limits of 58.82nM and 0.236µM with the corresponding linear ranges of 25–200µM and 25–300µM, respectively. According to this phenomenon, an “AND” logic gate based on the novel N-CDs has been constructed. As-prepared N-CDs with negligible toxicity and perfect biocompatibility were expanded for cellular imaging and sensing Fe3+ and AA in living cell, which enlarges the application range of the N-CDs. Most importantly, the as-constructed fluorescent sensing system was successfully applied to detection of Fe3+ in tap water and the analyses of AA in fresh fruits with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) as a class of unique fluorescent materials have attracted tremendous attention due to its high optical absorptivity, resistance to photobleaching, chemical stability, biocompatibility and low toxicity [1–5]. The applications of CDs have been extended in biomedical imaging [6,7], drug delivery [8,9], fluorescence sensors [10–12], photocatalysis [13,14]. The CDs generally consist of three elements: carbon, hydrogen and oxygen and possess nonperfect sp2 domains, which can give rise to defects and surface states, resulting in broad and low-energy photoluminescence emission. Therefore, the CDs usually suffer from poor luminescence efficiency and further limit their utilization. Heteroatom doping of CDs with non-metallic elements is an effective approach to improve the photoluminescence properties of CDs [15–19]. Nitrogen (N) is considered to be a prominent chemical doping element for tailoring the electronic properties of CDs due to the close similarity of atomic size and strong valence bonds with carbon atoms. The presence of the additional lone pairs of electrons and the commonly introducing defects sites from N doping would also provide possible active sites and modify the local chemical activities of CDs [20–23]. N-doping introduces emissive energy traps for the sake of facilitating the emissive recombination of localized electron–hole pairs [24]. It provides the better understanding for the enhancement fluorescence behavior of CDs by N-doping.

N-doped CDs enable construction of fluorescent nanosensors for the detection of various targets such as ions (Fe3+ [25], Ag+ [26], Hg2+ [27], Cu2+ [28], Pb2+ [29], NO3 - [30], etc.), molecular substances (cysteine [31], ascorbic acid [32], glucose [33], amoxicillin [34], aflatoxin B1 [35], etc.). Numerous N-CD systems are reported for selective recognition of an individual analyte. Some attempts was made to the development of N-CD as a platform for multiple targets sensing. Wu et al. [36] demonstrated an “turn–on” approach for highly selective recognition of Hg2+ and Ag+ using N-CDs as a bifunctional sensing substrate based on ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) induced fluorescent recovery. Zhang et al. [37] also reported N-CDs as a dual-mode sensor to sequentially detect Hg2+ and L-cysteine because the interaction between Hg2+ and L-Cys leads Hg2+ to escape from the surface of N-CDs and form a Hg2+–S bond. Zhang et al. [38] further proposed a bifunctional fluorescence probe based on N-CDs for sequential determination of chromium Cr (VI) based on the inner filter effect (IFE) and ascorbic acid, because Cr(VI) can be reduced to Cr(III), resulting in the elimination of the IFE and recovery of CD fluorescence. Due to distinct physicochemical properties, N-CDs as an fluorescence sensing platform with different spectral signals can offer an opportunity to devise a single nanosensor for multiple targets [39–41]. Which prompted us to explore the latent applications.

Iron is the most essential trace metal element in all living organisms, which plays crucial roles in oxygen metabolism and electron transfer processes due to its facile redox chemistry and high affinity for oxygen [42,43]. Iron deficiency and overload can both disturb the cellular homeostasis and induce various biological disorders [44]. Therefore, determination of intracellular iron is of great significance in health monitoring. On the other hand, ascorbic acid (AA) or more well-known as vitamin C, is a kind of important nutrient that can only be supplied by food but is crucially important for human body. AA has been used for the prevention and treatment of the common cold, mental illness, infertility, and coronary heart diseases [45,46]. It is essential to develop a simple and rapid method for its determination in routine analysis.

Herein, we fabricated N-CDs through facile and economical one-step hydrothermal approach by using catechol and triethylene tetramine as raw material. Various characterizations indicate that N-CDs exhibit remarkably photostability under extreme pH, ionic strengths, and light illumination. The as-synthesised N-CDs have been successfully utilised to bitifunctional sensor for the detection of Fe3+ and AA ( Scheme 1) by an “on-off-on” fluorescence response. The fluorescence of N-CDs are prominently decreased in the presence of Fe3+ by the static quenching owing to forming a N-CDs-Fe3+ complex. The subsequent fluorescence restoration was induced with the addition of AA. Because Fe3+ undergoes reduction to Fe2+, resulting in the elimination of the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) and recovery of N-CDs fluorescence. This phenomenon also enabled N-CDs act as an accurate “on-off-on” fluorescence switch and the design of an “AND” logic gate. Most importantly, as-prepared N-CDs were successfully employed for cellular imaging Fe3+ and AA in living cell and the determination of Fe3+ in tap water and the analyses of AA in fresh fruits.

Catechol (99%), Triethylene tetramine, Ascorbic acid (AA) were obtained from Alfa Aesar (Shanghai, China). Other chemicals were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were used without further purification unless otherwise noted. Water was deionized and purified with a resistivity of 18.25MΩcm by being passed through a Milli-Q.

The N-CDs were synthesized based on our previous method with a slight change. In brief, 0.11g catechol (99%) was dissolved into 20mL of ultrapure water in a 50mL beaker, and 100µL of triethylene tetramine was added to the above solution and stirred under ultrasonic stirring for 30min to form a homogeneous solution. The above reaction mixture was transferred into a 50mL teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200℃ for 10h. After that, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, and then resultant brown-yellow mixture was centrifuged by High-speed centrifuge (TGL-18C, China) to remove the large particles at 12500rpm for 15min. Finally, the purification of the supernatant was dialyzed in dialysis tubing with MWCO of 1000Da (Spectra/Por 6, Spectrum Laboratories, CA, USA) for roughly 24h to remove impurities. The obtained brown-yellow solution of CDs was stored at 4℃ for subsequent experiments.

Fluorescence measurements were conducted on a spectrophotomer (Varian Cary Eclipse). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) were performed on a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, Worcestershire, U.K.). Optical absorption spectra were recorded on a Lambda 365 spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, Llantrisant, U.K.). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and (HR-TEM) images were acquired on a FEI Tecnai G2F20 with an accelerating voltage of 200kV. The TEM specimens were prepared by placing one drop of the CDs solution on carbon coated copper grid and were dried at room temperature. Bruker Tensor II Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer was applied to analyze the chemical bonding information of as-constructed CDs on the basis of KBr pellets. The Thermo Escalab 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA) X-ray was employed to characterize the content of elements and the chemical composition of the CDs. The XPS sample was prepared by attaching a small piece of double-sided adhesive to the CDs powder to the aluminum foil and then pressing the foil and placing it on a tabletting machine to a pressure of 6MPa for 1min. The compositions of the CDs was characterized by elemental analysis, which was performed on an Elementar Vario Micro Cube (Germany). Elemental analysis sample was prepared by grinding, weighing and then wrapping them with a tin foil. The fluorescence life time was measured with a FLS 920 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd). A FE20 pH meter (Mettler Toledo, Switzerland) was used to adjust the pH value. Images were obtained using a confocal laser scanning microscope (LSM880+Airyscan, Zeiss).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorimetry for the determination of Fe3+and AA
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of N-CD in 0.01M PBS buffer solution (pH 7.4) was 2mgmL−1. Fe3+ and other cations stock solutions were prepared at 10mM by dissolving appropriate amounts of metal salts in ultrapure water. 0.2mL of the above metal ion solution was well mixed with 1.8mL of the N-CD solution by gentle shaking, and the PL intensities were measured. For fluorescence titrations of N-CDs with Fe3+, 1.8mL of the above buffer solution containing requisite volume of N-CDs was added an appropriate aliquot of Fe3+ at different consistency, respectively. The fluorescence spectra were recorded under excitation at 415nm at a scan rate of 600nmmin−1. The slit width was set at 5nm for the excitation and 10nm for the emission.

For the determination of AA, Fe3+ (0.5mM) was added to 2mL of N-CDs (2mgmL−1) to form a N-CDs/Fe3+ mixture solution. Then 10µL various concentrations of AA were added, and the mixture was shaken thoroughly. The mixed solution was maintained at room temperature. After 2min, fluorescence recovery spectra were recorded. The selectivity of N-CDs/Fe3+ probe for AA was assessed by adding other interferants containing (DL-Cysteine (DL-Cys), L-Cysteine (L-Cys), aspartic acid (Asp), Phenylalanine (Phe), Dopamine (DA), Threonine (Thr), Glutamic acid (Gla), Glycine (Gly), Glucose(Glu), Citric acid (CA), glutathione (GSH), Bovine serum albumin (BSA), S2-, S2O32-, SO32-, NO2-, Br- and I-) instead of AA in a similar way.

The Human breast cancer MCF-7 cells were seeded in glass bottom culture dishes and grown in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2 at 37℃ in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin/streptomycin. After the removal of the DMEM, the MCF-7 cells are treated with N-CDs (600µgmL−1) and N-CDs/0.5mMMn+ (represents K+, Na+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Mn2+ and Fe2+), respectively for 2h at 37℃. Subsequently, the cells are washed twice with PBS (pH 7.4) to remove extracellular CDs and kept in PBS for optical imaging by a LSM880+Airyscan confocal laser scanning microscope (Carl Zeiss Co. Ltd.). Then the addition of Fe3+ with a concentration of 0.5mM, MCF-7 cell images and fluorescence intensity were monitored after reaction times 30min. Finally, the addition of AA with a concentration of 0.7mM, the MCF-7 cell images and fluorescence intensity were recorded after incubation for 30min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of the CDs based nanoprobe for the detection of Fe3+and AA in real samples, quantitative analysis of Fe3+ in the tap water and AA in fresh fruits were performed using the present CD-based sensor. The tap water samples were obtained from our lab and spiked with Fe3+ without any pretreatment. Fresh fruits (orange, red grape and kiwi) samples were purchased from a local supermarket in Taiyuan, China, and used immediately after treatment. Fresh fruits (orange, red grape and kiwi) samples were prepared according to the following approaches. First, all of the samples were washed with ultrapure water and dried naturally in air at room temperature. They were then accurately weighed, homogenized by using a commercial blender and centrifuged at 10℃ in a refrigerated centrifuge at 8000rpm for 10min. After original AA in the filtrates of fresh fruits were measured, exogenetic AA at different concentrations was added into the samples and detected using the proposed methods.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images presented in Fig. 1a reveals that as-synthesised N-CDs are uniform nano-dots with near spherical shape. Fig. 1b clearly confirms that N-CDs are mainly distributed in the narrow range of 4–7nm with a mean particle diameter of 5.38 ± 0.8nm (based on statistical analysis of approximately 100dots). TEM (HRTEM) inset in Fig. 1a shows high crystalline core of N-CDs with an interlayer spacing of about 0.21nm, which is much smaller spacing compared with graphite crystals (0.335nm) due to hybridization sp2 and sp3 among carbon atoms and nitrogen atoms [47]. The DLS result indicates that the average particle size of N-CDs is 8.77 ± 0.60nm (Fig. S1†). Obviously, hydrodynamic diameter is larger than that from the TEM. Because the hydrodynamic diameter includes all solvent molecules attracted to the surface of the CDs, which also suggests that N-CDs are highly dispersible in aqueous solution.

As shown in Fig. 2a, three dominant peaks at 282.8, 397.9 and 528.4eV in the XPS survey spectrum are attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum in Fig. 2b predominantly shows three peaks at 284.8, 285.7 and 286.4eV, which are associated with C1s states in C–C/C˭C, C–O–C/C–OH, C–N, respectively [28,48]. This indicates that the N-CDs are rich in hydrophilic groups on the surfaces, which is consistent with the corresponding FT-IR spectrum. The high-resolution N 1s XPS spectrum exhibits the distinctive peaks of at 399.8 and 401.6eV, representing N 1s states in pyrrolic-like N [23,49,50] and N-H (Fig. 2c) and implies that N has been partly doped into the CDs [17,51]. The O 1s spectrum could be decomposed into peaks at 532.2 and 533.0eV (Fig. S2†), suggesting the presence of C˭O and C–OH/C–O–C groups, respectively [52]. Table S1A† summaries the result of elemental analysis of N-CDs which coincides with that of XPS. Compared with previous reported papers, the doping contents of N and O are higher about 17.94 and 28.17, respectively, [53,54]. Table S1B† takes advantage of the relative number of atoms to express the elemental contents. The conventional formula for a N-CDs is nearly C10H20N3O5.

FT-IR spectroscopy was also employed to characterize N-CDs with the surface functional groups as shown in Fig. 2e. A broad absorption peak at 3396cm−1 is assigned to stretching vibrations of O–H or N–H bonds [55]. N-CDs shows that peaks at 2710cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibration of C–H [5]. The peaks at 1485cm−1 and 1370cm−1 can be attributed to characteristic stretching vibration band of the amine III C–N bond and C–N= [51]. The stretching vibrations of the C–O, C–N and C–O–C appear at 1258/1098, 1200 and 1033cm−1 [51,56]. The two characteristic peaks ascribed to C˭C stretching of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (1589cm−1) and N–H bending (1513cm−1) are observed. The surface components of the N-CDs determined by the FT-IR are consistent with XPS results.

Fig. 3a shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the N-CDs. As shown, the optical absorption peak of the N-CDs was observed in the UV region with a maximum absorption at 275nm, possibly attributed to the π−π* transition of the aromatic C˭C bond [41]. The maximum excitation and emission located at 415 and 515nm can be obtained depicted in Fig. 3. The photograph of the N-CDs aqueous solution under UV (365nm) exhibits bright green FL (inset in Fig. 3a). The emission spectra of NCDs excited at various wavelengths are shown in Fig. 3b, which present the common excitation-dependence emission behaviors of CDs. With increasing the excitation wavelength from 360 to 460nm, the emission peak red-shifts from 500 to 525nm. This behavior is ascribed to the complexity of surface excited states of the N-CDs [57,58], which is universal in fluorescent carbon materials [55]. Furthermore, the Φs is 19.3% for CDs (Fig. S3a†), using quinine sulfate in 0.1M sulfuric acid solution (quantum yield 54%) (Fig. S3b†) as the reference [59]. This quantum yield value is comparable to previous reports [41,55,60].

The functions of ionic strength, pH and sustained xenon arc light exposure were surveyed. As depicted in Fig. S4a†, FL intensity or peak characteristic remains unvarying under different concentrations of KCl (0–2.5molL−1), indicating that N-CDs could be stable in a medium of comparatively high ionic strength. However, the FL intensities decrease under extreme pH conditions, remain constant at pH 4.0–9.0 (Fig. S4b†), indicating its potential application in a wide pH environment and cellular imaging (vide infra). As depicted in Fig. S4c†, no obvious change in FL intensity suggests that favorable photostability of the N-CDs after 120min exposure to xenon arc light.

To evaluate the selectivity of the present sensing system, the effect of common metal ions on CDs fluorescence quenching under the same conditions was investigated. As shown in Figure. S5†, the fluorescence response of CDs was studied in the presence of different metal ions at the concentration of 1mM. It was noticeable that Fe3+ and Ag+ can bring about the notable decrease of the fluorescence intensity. While the other metal ions showed only a slight quenching effect and the influence was almost negligible. Meanwhile, the anti-interference performance of the sensing system was also evaluated, some metal ions (K+, Na+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Al3+, Pb2+ and Ni2+) coexisting at a concentration of 20-fold of Fe3+ caused negligible fluorescence change, while others (Cu2+, Hg2+, Cr3+ and Fe2+) kept lower than 10-fold induced a little fluorescence quenching. Fe3+ is found to be much more responsive than Ag+, which clarify that the as-constructed CDs are highly selective toward Fe3+ over the other metal ions. To avoid interference from Ag+ at high concentrations, sodium thiosulfate as a masking agent enable Ag+ to be effectively eliminated (Figure. S6†). Herein, we conducted a detailed FL quenching study by Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 4a, upon addition of Fe3+ to N-CDs solution, the FL of N-CDs were rapidly and strongly quenched, accompanying by weak fluorescence color changes of the N-CDs (Fig. S7†). The UV–vis spectrum of the N-CDs also showed an apparent change upon the presence of Fe3+ (Fig. 4b, curve). Fe3+ caused an outstanding reduction in absorbance at 275nm and a distinct enhancement in absorption bands centered at 300–500nm. Meanwhile, the most likely explanation for the Fe3+-induced change in the UV–vis spectrum of N-CDs is that Fe3+ has an affinity between N or/and O on the surfaces of the N-CDs [61,62]. The absorption spectral changes enable the observation of a colorimetric switching from faint yellow to green for Fe3+ (Fig. S7†).

The concentration dependence binding of N-CDs with Fe3+ was evaluated by fluorescence titration experiments. Fig. 5 depicts the FL decrease of N-CDs by addition of incremental amount of Fe3+. The insets exhibit an excellent linearity for Fe3+ in the range of 25–200µM with a linear equation of y = 1.53x +107.7 (R2 = 0.9945). Here F0 and F are the fluorescent intensities of CDs at 515nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 58.82nM for Fe3+ according to the IUPAC 3s criterion (LOD=3SD/S, where SD is the standard error of the intercept and S is the slope of the calibration curve). The limit of quantification (LOQ), established for 10 times the standard deviation of the blank signal divided by the slope of the calibration curve was 202.93nM. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was of 4.67%. The above results implied that N-CDs showed very promising utilization in the determination of Fe3+. Moreover, the fluorescence decay dynamics was used to reveal the FL quenching mechanism of N-CDs in the “on–off” process. As shown in Fig. S8† and Table S2†, a two-component decay-time model of N-CDs, N-CDs/Fe3+originated a good fit with the average lifetimes of 4.71ns and 4.36ns. the Their lifetimes present a slight change, implying that the quenching mechanism of CDs in the presence of Fe3+ is static quenching [63,64], which attribute to the good affinity between Fe3+ and the functional groups of CDs [40,65,66].

N-CDs could be directly employed as a sensing platform via the “on-off” process for Fe3+. Subsequently, we found that the addition of AA could recovery fluorescence quenched by Fe3+. Therefore, the N-CDs-Fe3+ system could behave as a “off-on” FL nanoprobe for detection of AA. As illustrated in a proposed “on−off−on” sensing process ( Fig. 6a), the colors of N-CDs, N-CDs/Fe3+ and N-CDs/Fe3+/AA solution varied under irradiation with daylight and a 365nm UV lamp. Upon the addition of AA to the N-CDs-Fe3+ mixture, the quenched FL of the N-CDs was restored immediately within 2min (Fig. S9†). After 2min, the fluorescence signal was stabilized, indicating a fast recovery of the system. Fig. 6b shows that the FL intensity of N-CDs gradually enhanced with increasing concentrations of AA. From the inset of Fig. 6b, a good linear relationship between F0-F and AA concentration is observed from 25 to 300µM with a linear equation of y = 0.38 AA + 1.51 (R2 = 0.994). The LOD of AA is 0.236µM based on the IUPAC 3s criterion. The proposed sensing principle for AA based on N-CDs-Fe3+ system is illustrated in Scheme 2. AA easily attacks the positively charged Fe3+ and reduces the oxidation state of the cations from Fe3+ to Fe2+, suppressing the quenching process between Fe3+ and N-CDs [63,64]. As a result, the complexation between Fe3+ and N-CDs will be disrupted and restoration of N-CDs fluorescence can take place. To further evaluate selectivity of the N-CDs-Fe3+ probe for determination of AA, the potential interferences were tested. As shown in Fig. 6c, under the same test conditions, even when the interferences’ concentrations were 2 times higher than that of the analyte AA, they did not interfere with detection of AA. Meanwhile, the anti-interference performance of the sensing system was also evaluated, as presented in Fig. 6d, with the addition of a mixture consisting of equal concentrations of AA and other potential interferences into the N-CDs-Fe3+ system. Compared with addition of only AA into the same sensing system, little changes in the FL are observed. These results prove that this determination system presents an outstanding selectivity and strong resistance ability toward AA sensing.

Based on the significant fluorescence restoration of the N-CDs-Fe3+ system in the simultaneous presence of AA, an “AND” molecular logic gate was developed ( Fig. 7). In this logic gate, Fe3+ and AA were acted as inputs accompanying by the fluorescent state of “off” and “on” and restored FL intensity at 515nm taken as the output. With respect to input in truth table (Fig. 7), the presence and absence of Fe3+and AA are assigned as “1” and “0”, respectively. For output, we define the strong FL change as “1” and weak FL change as “0”. In the absence of both inputs (0/0) and with Fe3+ (1, 0) or AA (0, 1) input alone, the output was “0”. Conversely, only in the existence of both Fe3+and AA (1, 1), the gate (output) was activated and offered an output signal of “1”.

For further biological applications, the cytotoxic effect of the FL probes to MCF-7 cells was evaluated by the conventional MTT assay. As shown in Fig. 8, MCF-7 cells were incubated with various N-CDs contents for 24h, respectively. The viability of the cells remains higher than 88% upon addition of the N-CDs over a wide concentration range of 25–600µgmL−1. In order to test ability of N-CDs to label vitro imaging of living cells, experiments were carried out to further demonstrate the availability of the N-CDs for imaging intracellular detection. Fig. S10 shows the bright-field images of the MCF-7 cells incubated with N-CDs under laser excitation at 488nm. No significant changes in cell morphology confirmed the negligible cytotoxicity of N-CDs. In addition, with setting up the excitation wavelength at 405nm, 488nm and 561nm, the emission color changes to blue-green-red suggesting the multicolor emission properties of the cells stained with N-CDs under excitation in the visible range (Fig. S11). Bestowed with the unique FL emission and good biocompatibility, the N-CDs are proposed to serve as a potentially multicolor bio-labeling reagent. We next assessed the ability of the nanoprobe to image Fe3+and AA in cell, when some essential ions are present in cell. Fig. 9 shows the LSCM images of the MCF-7 cells initially incubated with 0.60mgmL−1 N-CDs/0.5mMMn+ (Mn+ including (1) blank, (2) K+, (3) Na+, (4) Zn2+, (5) Mg2+, (6) Cu2+, (7) Ca2+, (8) Mn2+ and (9) Fe2+) at 37℃ for 2h, then exposed to Fe3+ for 30min, respectively, and finally incubated with AA solution for 30min. Fig. 9, 1A, N-CDs have good cell-membrance permeability, and the cells stained with N-CDs display strongest green emissions. From Fig. 9, 2A -9A, each graph is compared with Fig. 9, 1A, some essential ions with the concentration of 0.5mM do not affect the green emission of N-CDs in the cells, indicating that these the test ions at the same concentration level as that of Fe3+ do not quench the fluorescence of N-CDs. When Fe3+ is added into cells culture fluid, green emissions were weakened in (Fig. 9, 1B-9B). However, when AA is added into cells culture fluid, the green FL are recovered as shown in (Fig. 9, 1C-9C), demonstrating the recoverability of emission of N-CDs in the cells and other ions do not induce any FL change. Fig. 9D depicted the change of intracellular fluorescence intensities of 0.60mgmL−1 N-CDs/0.5mMMn+ (Mn+ including blank, K+, Na+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Mn2+ and Fe2+) without and with Fe3+ and AA, respectively. The intensities of 25 cells were measured. The λex/λem are 488/500 ± 25nm. The order of the cell emission intensity is that N-CDs are close to N-CDs/0.5mMMn+ > N-CDs/0.5mMMn+ /Fe3+/0.7mM AA > N-CDs/0.5mMMn+ /Fe3+. These observation further confirm that N-CDs are superior FL probes for “on-off-on” detecting intracellular Fe3+ and AA.

The feasibility of our nominated CDs-based fluorescence sensing for Fe3+ and AA in the real sample has been tested according to the proposed method. As presented in Table 1, the recoveries for the selective detection of Fe3+ in the tap water were in the range 96.49–104.12% and the RSD was in the range of 3.29–6.39%. Application of the N-CDs nanoprobe for AA sensing in various kinds of fruits samples was investigated further. As shown in Table 2, the amounts of AA in some fruits, such as orange, purple grape and kiwi, detected by the fabricated “off-on” sensor, were 39.16, 17.44 and 67.89mg/100g, respectively. It is worth noting that quantitative spike recoveries for the detection of AA in these above real samples ranged from 95.63% to 104.27%, with a relative standard deviation of less than 6.71% (n=5). These results further suggested that the N-CDs probe for monitoring Fe3+ in the tap water and AA in practical fruits samples was reliable and feasible.

In summary, catechol and triethylene tetramine were employed as precursors facilitating the formation of remarkably stable N-CDs with a Φs as high as 19.3%. The as-prepared N-CDs exhibit excellent chemical and optical stabilities. These properties enable development of N-CDs-based bifunctional FL probe for determination of Fe3+ and AA. The combination of Fe3+ and functional group on the surface of the N-CDs result in fluorescence quenching. The addition of AA could recovery fluorescence of N-CDs quenched by Fe3+, which was taken advantage of to realize the sensitive quantitation of AA. Bestowed with numerous superiorities, the fluorescent nanoprobe are successfully employed for construction of molecular logic gates and imaging Fe3+ and AA in living cell with excellent biocompatibility and low cell toxicity. Notably, the fabricated fluorescent bifunctional sensing system has been successfully used for the analysis of Fe3+ in tap water and AA in some fresh fruits. It is believed that N-CDs behave as an underlying candidate in monitoring the intracellular species, food chemistry and biomedical fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21705101, 21475080 and 21575084) and Shanxi Province Hundred Talents Project.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2017.11.030.
